name: Mymerak gravel route of the monasteries and pilgrims
name_original: Ruta Mymerak gravel de los monasterios y peregrinos
description:
The Route, which starts from Ventosa, has 77 kilometers and 1,100 meters of positive elevation gain and offers you a tour of the Rioja Alta region. In its first part, the route follows the French Camino de Santiago to the west, until it turns south, to visit the two monasteries located in San Millán de la Cogolla. The return is made through the towns of Badarán and Cárdenas, until returning to Ventosa.

You leave Ventosa to the northwest to immediately take the French Camino de Santiago in a westerly direction to Cirueña. At the height of Nájera you briefly leave the Camino to enter the town and when you leave it enjoy a path that runs parallel to the Najerilla river.

In Cirueña the route heads south, in a gentle ascent towards the first foothills of the Sierra de la Demanda. The climb is progressive, with few hard ramps. Two magnificent monasteries await you in San Millán de la Cogolla, Suso and Yuso.

Before going back to the east, there is one last climb to Alto de Villaverde to face 10 kilometers of descent between cultivated fields. Halfway down this section of descent you pass through Badarán, a good place to get supplies and prepare for the final section of the Route.

Once past Cárdenas, where it is advisable to stop at its source, you cross the Najerilla River again and begin a gentle ascent towards Cerro Alto. Once you overcome this last obstacle, you will only have three kilometers left until the end of the route in Ventosa.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Ventosa, La Rioja, offers diverse terrain characterized by rolling hills, expansive vineyards, and forests. Situated in the foothills of the Sierra de Moncalvillo, the landscape combines sierra and valley elements, with elevations reaching up to 642 meters. The region features ancient Roman roads and paths through scrubland, providing varied surfaces for gravel exploration. Best gravel bike trails around Ventosa The most popular gravel bike trail is Mymerak gravel route of the monasteries and pilgrims, a 48.0 miles (77.3 km) trail that takes about 7 hours 10 minutes to complete. This difficult route traverses historical paths and offers extensive views of the surrounding landscape. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is La Grajera Reservoir – Camino de Santiago loop from Navarrete, a moderate 28.1 miles (45.3 km) path. This route follows segments of the historic Camino de Santiago, offering a mix of cultural and natural scenery. Local gravel bikers also love the IglesiaDeSantaMariaLaMayor – Elciego Wineries loop from Cenicero, a 42.2 miles (68.0 km) trail leading through vineyards and past significant landmarks, often completed in about 4 hours 20 minutes. Gravel biking around Ventosa is defined by vineyard paths, rolling hills, and sections of the Camino de Santiago. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels, catering to various rider preferences. The routes in Ventosa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 79 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Ventosa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many gravel bike trails are available around Ventosa?
A: There are over 10 gravel bike trails around Ventosa, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored these routes extensively, with over 79 gravel bikers using them.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on gravel bike trails around Ventosa?
A: The terrain around Ventosa, La Rioja, is characterized by rolling hills, expansive vineyards, and forests. You'll find a mix of surfaces, including ancient Roman roads and paths through scrubland, providing diverse conditions for gravel exploration. The landscape combines sierra and valley elements, with elevations reaching up to 642 meters.

Q: Are there any family-friendly gravel routes in Ventosa?
A: While the guide primarily lists moderate to difficult routes, the region's diverse terrain means there are likely segments suitable for families, especially on flatter vineyard paths or sections of the Camino de Santiago. For a moderate option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741010" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Grajera Reservoir – Camino de Santiago loop from Navarrete&lt;/a&gt;, which is 28.1 miles long.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural features to see along the gravel routes?
A: Ventosa's gravel routes offer access to several interesting sights. You might encounter the historic Camino de Santiago, vineyards, and natural monuments. Highlights in the wider area include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4509944" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Osborne Bull&lt;/a&gt;, the striking &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5323862" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Red Rocks of Nájera&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3764706" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cliffs of Lapuebla de Labarca&lt;/a&gt;. For panoramic views, look out for viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4579884" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;LasNeveras&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4067711" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Moncalvillo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular gravel routes available?
A: Yes, many of the gravel routes around Ventosa are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741010" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Grajera Reservoir – Camino de Santiago loop from Navarrete&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate option. Another challenging circular route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38140610" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Grajera Park – San Antón Hill loop from Navarrete&lt;/a&gt;, covering 33.6 miles.

Q: What is the best time of year to go gravel biking in Ventosa?
A: La Rioja, where Ventosa is located, is known for its pleasant climate. While specific recommendations depend on personal preference, spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for gravel biking, allowing you to enjoy the vineyards and natural landscapes without extreme heat or cold. The region is recognized as one of the best areas in Spain for gravel riding, suggesting good conditions for much of the year.

Q: Where can I find parking or access trails via public transport?
A: Ventosa is a village on the Camino de Santiago, which implies a degree of accessibility. While specific parking areas for gravel trails aren't detailed, villages along the Camino often have public parking. For public transport, regional bus services might connect Ventosa to larger towns like Logroño, from where you could start your ride. It's advisable to check local transport schedules for the most current information.

Q: What do other gravel bikers say about the trails in Ventosa?
A: The gravel biking routes in Ventosa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 79 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Ventosa's varied terrain, often praising the scenic vineyard paths, rolling hills, and sections of the historic Camino de Santiago.

Q: Are there options for more challenging gravel rides with significant elevation gain?
A: Absolutely. Ventosa offers several difficult gravel routes with substantial elevation gain. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050776156/mymerak-gravel-route-of-the-monasteries-and-pilgrims" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mymerak gravel route of the monasteries and pilgrims&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 48.0-mile trail with over 1,100 meters of elevation gain. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741780" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vineyards – La Grajera Park loop from Cenicero&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 40.6 miles with over 635 meters of ascent.

Q: Can I find routes that specifically pass through vineyards?
A: Yes, gravel biking around Ventosa is notably defined by its vineyard paths. The region is part of the renowned Rioja wine area, and many routes wind through expansive vineyards. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23742644" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;IglesiaDeSantaMariaLaMayor – Elciego Wineries loop from Cenicero&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, leading through vineyards and past significant landmarks.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the trails?
A: Ventosa is a common stop for pilgrims on the Camino Francés, meaning the village itself offers basic amenities and likely cafes or small eateries. While specific establishments along the trails are not listed, many routes pass through or near other villages in the Rioja region, providing opportunities for refreshments. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer routes.

Q: What are the typical distances and durations for gravel rides in Ventosa?
A: Gravel rides in Ventosa vary in distance and duration to suit different preferences. Moderate routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741010" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Grajera Reservoir – Camino de Santiago loop from Navarrete&lt;/a&gt;, are around 28 miles and take about 4 hours 50 minutes. More difficult trails, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050776156/mymerak-gravel-route-of-the-monasteries-and-pilgrims&lt;/a&gt;, can be up to 48 miles and require over 7 hours to complete.
tips:
The guardaviñas are small stone huts used by farmers as shelter. As is the case, they are usually circular constructions that taper at the top using the "false dome" technique: raising successive rows of stones in increasingly narrow concentric circles.

This shack is located in Poyo Roldán (Alesón, La Rioja) in a small rest area next to the Camino de Santiago. Legend has it that the battle between Roland, nephew of Emperor Charlemagne, and the giant Ferragut, from Syria, took place here.
Over the Yalde river, we find a small wooden bridge surrounded by trees. The Camino de Santiago crosses it between the towns of Nájera and Ventosa.
If it has rained, be careful with the mud: it can be slippery at some point.

The rolls are stone columns, usually topped with a cross, which served as the insignia of a town's jurisdiction. They also served as a pillory, that is, it was the place where the severed heads of the executed were placed.

The roll of Azofra was granted by Carlos V, so that the inhabitants of Azofra had to be judged in Azofra itself.

Suso (from the Latin sursum) means "up", and is how this oldest monastery is known. Yuso (from the Latin deorsum) means “below” and is the other name by which the more modern Monastery of San Millán de la Cogolla is known.

Both were declared in 1997 by UNESCO, World Heritage, World Heritage. It is an exciting place, full of history and mystery. The guided tour (€4) includes the bus ride.

The complex formed by the monasteries of Suso (high) and Yuso (low) was declared a World Heritage Site by UNESCO in 1997. Its value lies not only in its architectural heritage, with its Gothic cloister or royal sarcophagi, but also in its cultural heritage by housing the codex where a monk wrote the first words in Spanish.

The first monastery dates back to the 11th century, but the complex and the entire town have evolved over the centuries until reaching the splendor and atmosphere that they offer today.

The perfect prelude to Sierra de la Demanda, San Millán de la Cogolla and Rioja wine. Badarán is a small agricultural enclave dedicated to viticulture and viniculture since, in addition to having vineyards around it, it houses several wineries that sell all kinds of wines directly to the public.
The Cárdenas fountain is a good place to stop to refuel if you are pedaling. Depending on the time of year in which you are doing the route, you will appreciate more or less this fresh water stop.

waypoints.name: San Millán de la Cogolla Monastery
